section.title: The Tenants, the Questions, and the Widow's Mite
section.sectionRationale: In the temple, Jesus tells the parable of the wicked tenants, exposing the leaders as those who reject the owner's servants and finally his beloved son. A barrage of hostile questions follows, on taxes, resurrection, and the greatest commandment, each turned back with wisdom, until no one dares ask more. Jesus then asks His own question about the Christ as David's Lord, warns against the scribes, and commends a poor widow whose two coins outweigh the rich.
section.thesis: Jesus is the beloved Son and rejected cornerstone whom the tenants kill, the Lord greater than David, who silences every hostile question, sums up the law as love for God and neighbour, and measures true giving not by amount but by the whole-hearted devotion of a poor widow.
section.frameNote: Six beats: the parable of the wicked tenants; the question about taxes to Caesar; the Sadducees on the resurrection; the greatest commandment; the Christ as David's Lord; and the warning about the scribes with the widow's offering.

units.label: The parable of the wicked tenants

verses.text: Then Jesus began to speak to them in parables: “A man planted a {{ampelon|vineyard}}. He put a wall around it, dug a wine vat, and built a watchtower. Then he rented it out to some tenants and went away on a journey.
At harvest time, he sent a servant to the tenants to collect his share of the fruit of the vineyard.
But they seized the servant, beat him, and sent him away empty-handed.
Then he sent them another servant, and they struck him over the head and treated him shamefully.
He sent still another, and this one they killed. He sent many others; some they beat and others they killed.
Finally, having one beloved son, he sent him to them. ‘They will respect my son,’ he said.
But the tenants said to one another, ‘This is the heir. Come, let us kill him, and the inheritance will be ours.’
So they seized the son, killed him, and threw him out of the vineyard.
What then will the owner of the vineyard do? He will come and kill those tenants and give the vineyard to others.
Have you never read this Scripture: ‘The stone the builders rejected has become the {{gonias|cornerstone}}.
This is from the Lord, and it is marvelous in our eyes’?”
At this, the leaders sought to arrest Jesus, for they knew that He had spoken this parable against them. But fearing the crowd, they left Him and went away.

units.label: Paying taxes to Caesar
units.note: give to God what is God's

verses.text: Later, they sent some of the Pharisees and Herodians to catch Jesus in His words.

“Teacher,” they said, “we know that You are honest and seek favor from no one. Indeed, You are impartial and teach the way of God in accordance with the truth. Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar or not? Should we pay them or not?”
But Jesus saw through their hypocrisy and said, “Why are you testing Me? Bring Me a denarius to inspect.”
So they brought it, and He asked them, “Whose image is this? And whose inscription?” “Caesar’s,” they answered.
Then Jesus told them, “Give to Caesar what is Caesar’s, and to God what is God’s.” And they marveled at Him.

units.label: The Sadducees and the resurrection
units.note: He is not the God of the dead, but of the living

verses.text: Then the Sadducees, who say there is no resurrection, came to Jesus and questioned Him:
“Teacher, Moses wrote for us that if a man’s brother dies and leaves a wife but no children, the man is to marry his brother’s widow and raise up offspring for him.
Now there were seven brothers. The first one married and died, leaving no children.
Then the second one married the widow, but he also died and left no children. And the third did likewise.
In this way, none of the seven left any children. And last of all, the woman died.
In the resurrection, then, whose wife will she be? For all seven were married to her.”
Jesus said to them, “Aren’t you mistaken, because you do not know the Scriptures or the power of God?
When the dead rise, they will neither marry nor be given in marriage. Instead, they will be like the angels in heaven.
But concerning the dead rising, have you not read about the burning bush in the Book of Moses, how God told him, ‘I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob’?
He is not the God of the dead, but of the living. You are badly mistaken!”
Now one of the scribes had come up and heard their debate. Noticing how well Jesus had answered them, he asked Him, “Which commandment is the most important of all?”
Jesus replied, “This is the most important: ‘Hear O Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is One.
{{agapao|Love}} the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind and with all your strength.’
The second is this: ‘Love your neighbor as yourself.’ No other commandment is greater than these.”
“Right, Teacher,” the scribe replied. “You have stated correctly that God is One and there is no other but Him,
and to love Him with all your heart and with all your understanding and with all your strength, and to love your neighbor as yourself. This is more important than all burnt offerings and sacrifices.”
When Jesus saw that the man had answered wisely, He said, “You are not far from the kingdom of God.” And no one dared to question Him any further.
While Jesus was teaching in the temple courts, He asked, “How can the scribes say that the Christ is the Son of David?
Speaking by the Holy Spirit, David himself declared: ‘The {{kyrios|Lord}} said to my Lord, “Sit at My right hand until I put Your enemies under Your feet.”’
David himself calls Him ‘Lord.’ So how can He be David’s son?” And the large crowd listened to Him with delight.

units.label: The scribes and the widow's offering
units.note: she gave all she had to live on

verses.text: In His teaching Jesus also said, “Watch out for the scribes. They like to walk around in long robes, to receive greetings in the marketplaces,
and to have the chief seats in the synagogues and the places of honor at banquets.
They defraud widows of their houses, and for a show make lengthy prayers. These men will receive greater condemnation.”
As Jesus was sitting opposite the treasury, He watched the crowd putting money into it. And many rich people put in large amounts.
Then one poor widow came and put in two small copper coins, which amounted to a small fraction of a denarius.
Jesus called His disciples to Him and said, “Truly I tell you, this poor widow has put more than all the others into the treasury.
For they all contributed out of their surplus, but she out of her poverty has put in all she had to live on.”

ampelon.gloss: A vineyard. In Isaiah 5 the vineyard is Israel, planted and tended by God. Jesus takes up that image: the owner is God, the tenants are Israel's leaders, the servants are the prophets, and the beloved son is Jesus, so the parable is a history of God's patience and Israel's rebellion.

gonias.gloss: The head of the corner, the cornerstone or capstone that holds a structure together. Quoting Psalm 118, Jesus identifies Himself as the stone the builders, the leaders, reject, whom God nonetheless makes the cornerstone of His new building through the resurrection.

agapao.gloss: To love with the whole self, a love of the will and the affections. The greatest commandment is to love God with all one's heart, soul, mind, and strength, and the second, to love one's neighbour as oneself. All the law hangs on this wholehearted love.

kyrios.gloss: Lord, master, the term the Greek Old Testament uses for God's name. In Psalm 110 David calls the Messiah my Lord, so the Christ is not merely David's descendant but David's Lord, greater than David, a hint of the divine dignity of the Son of David.

basic.context: Jesus tells a parable: a man plants a vineyard, rents it to tenants, and sends servants for the fruit, but the tenants beat and kill them, and finally kill the owner's beloved son to seize the inheritance. The owner will destroy them and give the vineyard to others. The leaders know it is about them. Then come three trap questions. On taxes, Jesus says give to Caesar what bears Caesar's image, and to God what is God's. To the Sadducees who deny resurrection, He shows from Moses that God is the God of the living. To a sincere scribe, He names the greatest commands: love God wholly and your neighbour as yourself. Then Jesus asks how the Christ can be both David's son and David's Lord. He warns against the showy, greedy scribes, and finally commends a poor widow who gives two tiny coins, all she had, more than all the rich.
basic.hermeneutics: The parable is a compressed history of salvation and a veiled announcement of Jesus' own death and vindication. The three questions show Jesus' wisdom silencing every faction. The greatest commandment gathers the whole law into love. The Christ-as-David's-Lord question presses His true identity. The widow closes the temple scenes with the true measure of devotion: not the size of the gift but the heart behind it.
basic.originalLanguages: The ampelon, vineyard, is Israel; the beloved son is Jesus, the rejected stone made the gonias, cornerstone. The greatest command is to agapao, love, God wholly and neighbour as self. And David, by the Spirit, calls the Christ kyrios, Lord.
basic.typology: The vineyard fulfils Isaiah 5; the rejected cornerstone fulfils Psalm 118. The Son sent last and killed is Jesus. The Christ as David's Lord in Psalm 110 points to His enthronement at God's right hand.
basic.christ: Jesus is the beloved Son sent last and killed, the rejected stone God makes the cornerstone, and the Lord greater than David enthroned at God's right hand. He is the wisdom that silences every challenge.
basic.god: God is the patient owner who sends servant after servant and at last His Son. God is the God of the living, whose power raises the dead. God claims wholehearted love and weighs the heart, not the amount, of every gift.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 5 gives the vineyard song. Psalm 118 and Psalm 110 supply the cornerstone and David's Lord. Deuteronomy 6 and Leviticus 19 give the two great commands.
basic.oneStory: The long story of God's patience with a rebellious people reaches its climax: He sends His beloved Son, whom the tenants kill, but God makes the rejected stone the cornerstone of a new people. The Christ is David's Lord, enthroned at God's right hand, and the true worshippers are not the showy scribes but a poor widow who gives her all, a picture of the whole-hearted love the law requires and the cross supremely displays.

context.literary: Chapter 12 continues the temple confrontations. The parable of the tenants answers the authority challenge of chapter 11 by exposing the leaders. Then Mark arranges a set of controversy dialogues with the major groups, Pharisees and Herodians, Sadducees, and a scribe, ending with Jesus on the offensive. The widow's offering, contrasted with the greedy scribes, closes the section and precedes the prediction of the temple's fall.
context.historical: The vineyard with wall, vat, and tower matches Isaiah 5 and ordinary Judean agriculture, where absentee landlords rented to tenants. The tax question was a trap: endorsing the poll tax angered nationalists, rejecting it invited a charge of sedition. The Sadducees accepted only the Pentateuch and denied resurrection. The temple treasury had trumpet-shaped receptacles for offerings; the widow's two lepta were the smallest coins in circulation.
context.canonical: The parable draws directly on the vineyard song of Isaiah 5:1-7 and climaxes with Psalm 118:22-23, the rejected stone become cornerstone, applied to Christ in Acts 4:11 and 1 Peter 2:7. The greatest commandment joins the Shema of Deuteronomy 6:4-5 with Leviticus 19:18. The Christ as David's Lord quotes Psalm 110:1, the most-cited Old Testament verse in the New Testament, grounding Jesus' enthronement.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the leaders as rebellious tenants who reject God's Son, to display Jesus' wisdom silencing every hostile question, to gather the law into wholehearted love, to press the true identity of the Christ as David's Lord, and to hold up a poor widow as the model of genuine devotion over showy religion.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parable and controversies describe Jesus' unique confrontation with the leaders and reveal His identity. The greatest commandment is permanently prescriptive: love for God and neighbour. The widow's example commends whole-hearted, sacrificial giving as a heart posture, not a fixed rule about amounts.

debates.question: What does give to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's mean?

views.label: Legitimate civil obligation under God's supremacy
views.case: The coin bears Caesar's image, so taxes may rightly be paid to the governing authority; yet human beings bear God's image, so our whole selves belong to God, who stands above every earthly ruler.

views.label: A refusal to let politics trap the gospel
views.case: Jesus neither endorses a nationalist revolt nor makes the kingdom a mere prop for Rome; He affirms limited civic duty while insisting that ultimate allegiance belongs to God alone, escaping the trap set for Him.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that earthly authorities have their proper due, but God's claim over the whole person is supreme, ordering all lesser loyalties.

debates.question: How does Jesus prove the resurrection from the burning bush?

views.label: God's covenant with the patriarchs implies their life
views.case: God says I am, not I was, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; since He is the God of the living and remains in covenant with them, they must live to Him, so the dead will rise.

views.label: The power of God guarantees resurrection life
views.case: The Sadducees erred by knowing neither the Scriptures nor God's power; the God who spoke at the bush is mighty to raise the dead into a transformed, angel-like existence beyond marriage, so their riddle collapses.

debates.bearing: Both hold that the living God's covenant faithfulness and almighty power together secure the resurrection, which the Sadducees denied to their own great error.

study.originalLanguages: The ampelon, the vineyard, is unmistakably Israel from Isaiah 5, and Jesus casts God as the patient owner, the prophets as the beaten servants, and Himself as the beloved son sent last and murdered, yet vindicated as the gonias, the cornerstone, the rejected stone God exalts. Pressed by hostile questions, He gathers the whole law into the command to agapao, to love, God with all one's heart, soul, mind, and strength, and one's neighbour as oneself. Then He turns the tables, showing from Psalm 110 that David, by the Spirit, calls the Christ kyrios, Lord, so the Messiah is no mere descendant of David but David's Lord, greater than the great king, enthroned at God's right hand.

typology.fulfillment: God's planting and tending of His people, and their leaders' rebellion, fulfilled and judged in Jesus, Isaiah 5:1-7 and Mark 12:9.
Christ rejected by the builders and made the cornerstone of God's new people through the resurrection, Psalm 118:22 and 1 Peter 2:7.
Jesus enthroned at God's right hand, greater than David, Psalm 110:1 and Acts 2:34-36.

study.christ: The parable of the tenants is Jesus' own veiled account of His death: He is the owner's one beloved Son, sent last after the prophets, whom the tenants seize, kill, and cast out of the vineyard, only to be vindicated by God as the cornerstone the builders rejected. In the controversies He shows a wisdom none can trap, and He gathers the entire law into love for God and neighbour, the love He Himself will supremely enact at the cross. Then He reveals that the Christ is David's Lord, not merely his son, hinting at His divine dignity and His enthronement at God's right hand. The rejected Son is the exalted Lord.
study.god: God is the long-suffering owner of the vineyard, who sends messenger after messenger and finally His own beloved Son, bearing with a rebellious people to the very edge of judgment. God is the God of the living, whose covenant with the patriarchs and whose almighty power secure the resurrection the Sadducees denied. God claims the whole person in love, above all burnt offerings and sacrifices, and He weighs the heart behind every gift, seeing a destitute widow's two coins as more than all the rich threw in. This is a God of patience, power, and searching love, who exalts the humble and judges the proud.

crossReferences.note: The song of the vineyard God planted, which yielded only wild grapes, the backdrop to the parable of the tenants.
The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone, applied by Jesus to Himself.
Hear O Israel, love the LORD your God with all your heart, the first and greatest commandment.
The LORD said to my Lord, sit at My right hand, the ground of Jesus' question about the Christ as David's Lord.

study.oneStory: The whole history of God's patience with His people is compressed into one parable: He plants a vineyard, sends servant after servant, and at last His beloved Son, whom the tenants kill, only for God to make the rejected stone the cornerstone of a new people. In the temple Jesus silences every challenger, sums up the law as wholehearted love, and reveals that the Christ is David's own Lord, enthroned at God's right hand. And He ends by lifting up a poor widow's total gift over the showy religion of the scribes, a quiet picture of the self-giving love the law commands and the cross will perfectly display.

quiz.q: Who are the characters in the parable of the wicked tenants?

options.text: God the owner, Israel's leaders the tenants, the prophets the servants, and Jesus the beloved son.
options.note: The parable draws on Isaiah 5 and casts Jesus as the son whom the tenants kill.

options.text: A landowner and some dishonest farm workers, with no deeper meaning.
options.note: The leaders knew it was spoken against them; it is a pointed allegory of salvation history.

options.text: God and His rebellious people who reject His messengers and His Son.
options.note: True in outline, but it should specify the leaders as the tenants and Jesus as the beloved son.

options.text: The Romans as tenants oppressing Israel the vineyard.
options.note: The tenants are Israel's own leaders, not Rome; the parable indicts them.

quiz.q: What did Jesus mean by give to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's?

options.text: Civil authorities may be given their due, but human beings bear God's image and owe their whole selves to God, who is supreme.
options.note: Mark 12:17; the coin bears Caesar's image, but we bear God's, so God's claim is total.

options.text: That taxes should always be paid without question.
options.note: Jesus affirms limited civic duty, but the weight falls on giving our whole selves to God.

options.text: That earthly rulers have a proper claim while God's claim over us is ultimate.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should include that we bear God's image and owe Him our whole selves.

options.text: That God and Caesar have equal claims on us.
options.note: God's claim is supreme; Caesar's is limited and subordinate.

quiz.q: How did Jesus answer the Sadducees who denied the resurrection?

options.text: God told Moses He is the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and He is God of the living, so the dead will rise.
options.note: Mark 12:26-27; the covenant with the patriarchs implies their ongoing life.

options.text: He said they simply needed more faith.
options.note: He said they knew neither the Scriptures nor the power of God, and reasoned from Moses, not merely from faith.

options.text: He showed from Scripture and God's power that they were mistaken.
options.note: True, but it should name the specific proof from the burning bush and the God of the living.

options.text: He agreed marriage continues unchanged in the resurrection.
options.note: Jesus said the risen neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like the angels.

quiz.q: What did Jesus name as the greatest commandments?

options.text: To love God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength, and to love your neighbor as yourself.
options.note: Mark 12:29-31 joins the Shema with Leviticus 19:18.

options.text: To love God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength.
options.note: That is the first and greatest, but Jesus paired it with loving your neighbour as yourself.

options.text: To offer burnt offerings and sacrifices faithfully.
options.note: The scribe rightly noted love is more important than all offerings and sacrifices.

options.text: To keep the Sabbath above all other commands.
options.note: Jesus named love for God and neighbour as the greatest, not the Sabbath specifically.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus commend the poor widow's two small coins?

options.text: Because she gave out of her poverty all she had to live on, unlike the rich who gave from their surplus.
options.note: Mark 12:43-44; her gift was total devotion, measured by the heart, not the amount.

options.text: Because her gift was the largest sum given that day.
options.note: It was the smallest sum, yet the greatest gift, because it was everything she had.

options.text: Because she gave sacrificially rather than from what she could spare.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should contrast her total giving with the rich giving from surplus.

options.text: Because she gave more cheerfully than the others.
options.note: The text stresses that she gave all she had, not merely that she gave cheerfully.

questions: The tenants wanted the vineyard's fruit without the owner. Where are you tempted to enjoy God's gifts while resisting His claim on your life?
Jesus said we bear God's image and owe Him our whole selves. What would it look like to give God what is God's in a specific area you have held back?
The greatest command is to love God with everything. Which part, heart, soul, mind, or strength, do you most need to bring fully into your love for God?
The scribes loved recognition and long public prayers for show. Where might you be tempted to practice religion for the eyes of others?
The widow gave all she had to live on. What would whole-hearted, trusting generosity look like for you, beyond giving only from your surplus?

translationNotes.note: The cornerstone, literally the head of the corner, renders Psalm 118:22; whether a foundation stone or a capstone, it is the decisive stone the builders discarded and God exalted, applied to Christ.
The LORD said to my Lord quotes Psalm 110:1; the first Lord is God, the second the Messiah, so David, by the Spirit, calls his descendant his Lord, pointing to the Christ's greater dignity.
Two small copper coins renders two lepta, the smallest coins in use, together a tiny fraction of a day's wage; the point is that she gave everything she had.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 12:1-44. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem markers were dropped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
